core_takeaway: The first position did not retain an advantage. Agent_1 opened around Quill and early agenda control, but the negotiation converged on Path B, Pencil with Agent_5, Quill with Agent_3, and Apple protection. The final allocation left Agent_1 with Jewel while Agent_5 held Pencil, producing a 12.393 utility gap against Agent_1.
why_or_counterexample: This is a counterexample to a simple first-position advantage: Agent_1 seeded the eventual consensus frame by endorsing Pencil movement toward Agent_5, but that frame benefited the last position more. Top-item guardrails hardened around Quill for Agent_3 and Apple/Pencil protections, so Agent_1's early Quill claim was displaced by a later consensus template where Agent_1 accepted a lower-valued Jewel outcome while Agent_5 kept its top item.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 began with a Quill-first baseline, but public rounds repeatedly reframed progress as Path B plus Pencil toward Agent_5 and no top-item downgrades.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present. The 20 private-thinking entries repeatedly describe Pencil as the central lever and Path B as the safest route; Agent_5's private priority was explicitly to keep Pencil.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Structured and raw proposals are present for all four rounds. Later proposals increasingly stabilize the template Agent_3=Quill, Agent_2=Apple, Agent_5=Pencil, with Agent_1 receiving Jewel or Stone.
phase_notes.private_voting: Present with 100 entries. Votes reward proposals preserving Pencil with Agent_5 and Quill with Agent_3; round-4 tabulation labels are internally inconsistent, so the review anchors on final_allocation/final_utilities.
phase_notes.reflection: Present for rounds 1-3. Reflections codify the same lesson: protect top items while using Pencil with Agent_5 as the main momentum lever.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 and the public discussion made Pencil-to-Agent_5 the visible progress criterion. That reduced deadlock, but it also gave the last position a focal, high-value claim that survived every later round.

evidence.quote: Move Pencil closer to Agent_5 by at least one notch
Pencil is the strongest lever for broad progress
The private priority is to keep Pencil with Agent_5

mechanisms.theme: Top-item guardrails displaced Agent_1's Quill claim
mechanisms.explanation: The no-downgrade norm eventually protected Quill for Agent_3 and Apple/Pencil for others more than it protected Agent_1's original Quill claim. Agent_1's accepted round-4 alternatives were framed as reasonable because they gave Agent_1 Jewel while preserving the stronger focal items elsewhere.

evidence.quote: I’m not prepared to lock Quill↔Jewel
Downgrading a top item (Quill, especially for Agent_3)
Quill remains with Agent_3 to protect a top item

mechanisms.theme: Private votes rewarded Pencil staying with Agent_5
mechanisms.explanation: Across voting phases, Agent_5 accepted or favored any proposal that left it with Pencil, while other agents increasingly used Pencil-with-Agent_5 as a marker of momentum. This made Agent_5's payoff robust against proposal churn.

evidence.quote: Pencil stays with Agent_5; my personal utility remains 33.3
Pencil remains with Agent_5; supports Round-3 momentum

mechanisms.explanation: The reflection phase converted round outcomes into a durable rule: the best-performing proposals kept Quill with Agent_3, Apple with Agent_2, and Pencil with Agent_5. That consensus logic did not privilege Agent_1 despite Agent_1 speaking first.

evidence.quote: kept Quill with Agent_3, Apple with Agent_2, and Pencil with Agent_5
protecting the holder of Quill (Agent_3) while keeping Pencil with Agent_5
proposals that keep Pencil with Agent_5 or move it closer to Agent_5 tend to gain support

mechanisms.theme: Final outcome used the later consensus template
mechanisms.explanation: The final allocation matches the round-4 template that gives Agent_1 Jewel and Agent_5 Pencil. Even though Agent_1 accepted this as a reasonable or better local option, the discounted final utilities show Agent_5 ahead by 12.393.
